coredata.dc:title: Single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymer embedded carbon dot for fast detection of tetracycline in honey
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huiyu
Zhao, Li
Xu, Yuan
Zhou, Tianyu
Liu, Haochi
Huang, Ning
Ding, Jie
Li, Yi
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: It is difficult to detect tetracycline (TC) in honey sample by using carbon dots (CDs) because the autofluorescence of the matrix of honey sample overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the large majority of CDs. Herein, single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymers embedded carbon dots (HMIP@CD) was prepared via microwave-assisted method. TC in diluted honey sample was adsorbed by the HMIP@CD within 3 min, after which the HMIP@CD absorbed with TC was separated by centrifugation from honey sample and redispersed into phosphate buffer solution. The autofluorescence of honey that interferes with the fluorescence signal of HMIP@CD was avoided. The method exhibited an excellent linearity within 10–200 μg L−1 and a low detection limit of 3.1 μg L−1. At three spiking levels of TC, the recoveries ranged from 93% to 105% with precisions below 1.6%. This method provides an effective strategy for detecting analyte in complex matrix with autofluorescence interference.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Molecularly Imprinted Polymers
Hollow
Tetracycline
Honey
Autofluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AEAPMS functionalized carbon dots (AEAPMS-CD)
Preparation of carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for (A) preparation of HMIP@CD, (B) fluorescence detection process of TC in honey.
(A) TEM image of NSCD (inset: size distribution histogram of NSCD). (B) SEM image of HMIP@CD. (C) FT-IR spectrum of HMIP@CD. (D) XRD patterns of NSCD and HMIP@CD.

(A) Fluorescence response time of HMIP@CD and SMIP@CD for TC (100 μg L−1). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of HMIP@CD (a) and TC (b). Fluorescence emission spectra of HMIP@CD (c). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of HMIP@CD before (a) and after (b) template molecules extraction, HMIP@CD with addition of 100 μg L−1 TC (c), HMIP@CD mixed with TC-spiked honey sample (100 μg L−1) (d), and the separated and redispersed HMIP@CD after adsorption of TC (e). (D) Comparison of the Stern–Volmer plots for HMIP@CD with that for SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD. The concentration of HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD was 0.01 mg mL−1.
Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of HMIP@CD and HNIP@CD with addition of 100 μg L−1 tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), florfenicol (FF), enrofloxacin (ENR) and sulfamonomethoxine (SMM).
Quantitative detection results of TC in honey sample using HMIP@CD.
Comparison of the proposed method with other reported fluorescent methods for TC determination in different samples.

head.ce:title: Single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymer embedded carbon dot for fast detection of tetracycline in honey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, 202 Haihe Road, Nangang District, Harbin 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is difficult to detect tetracycline (TC) in honey sample by using carbon dots (CDs) because the autofluorescence of the matrix of honey sample overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the large majority of CDs. Herein, single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymers embedded carbon dots (HMIP@CD) was prepared via microwave-assisted method. TC in diluted honey sample was adsorbed by the HMIP@CD within 3 min, after which the HMIP@CD absorbed with TC was separated by centrifugation from honey sample and redispersed into phosphate buffer solution. The autofluorescence of honey that interferes with the fluorescence signal of HMIP@CD was avoided. The method exhibited an excellent linearity within 10–200 μg L−1 and a low detection limit of 3.1 μg L−1. At three spiking levels of TC, the recoveries ranged from 93% to 105% with precisions below 1.6%. This method provides an effective strategy for detecting analyte in complex matrix with autofluorescence interference.

Based on the method of separation and redispersion, the determination of tetracycline (TC) in honey sample was achieved by single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymers embedded carbon Dots (HMIP@CD).
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention because of their superior properties such as simple synthesis, good photostability, easy functionalization, excellent aqueous solubility, and great biocompatibility [1]. The non-toxic feature makes CDs a good alternative to dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. CDs have been increasingly applied to the detection of ions [2] and antibiotics [3] in real samples. However, CDs are often quenched by other species, which make it difficult to detect analyte in complex samples [4]. The other main obstacle is that the autofluorescence of some real samples overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, which further limits the application of CDs.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are well-established materials prepared in the presence of template molecules. Along with the removal of template molecules, cavities have the capacity to selectively rebind template molecules are formed inside of MIPs [5]. Molecularly imprinted polymers based on carbon dots (MIP@CD) exhibits sensitive detection, selective recognition and excellent anti-interference ability [6–10]. So far, there is some excellent work on the detection of tetracycline (TC) based on MIP@CD. Huang's group have prepared MIP@CD via reverse microemulsion method and applied it to the detection of TC in fish [11]. Our group have also successfully fabricated MIP@CD based on sol-gel method for the detection of TC in milk [12]. In these interesting works, the imprinted polymers are all solid core microsphere. Imprinting sites of solid core molecularly imprinted microsphere are not only located on the surface of the microsphere, but also dispersed within the microsphere, which makes the utilization of imprinting sites, adsorption rate and sensitivity not always satisfactory. Hollow structure of single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymers (HMIPs) enable imprinting sites to spread to the external and internal surface of HMIPs, which will enhance the binding efficiency. The combination of HMIPs and CDs can significantly improve the adsorption rate and sensitivity of the material. So far, there was no report about the combination of HMIPs and CDs.

Herein, single-hole hollow molecularly imprinted polymers embedded carbon dots (HMIP@CD) was prepared via microwave-assisted sol-gel method for the detection of TC in honey. CDs cannot directly applied to detect TC in honey, because the autofluorescence of honey overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs [13,14]. In experiment, the HMIP@CD was first dispersed in the diluted honey sample. After adsorption, the HMIP@CD was separated by centrifugation. Finally, the HMIP@CD was dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and followed by fluorescence detection. This method successfully avoids the interference from the autofluorescence of honey.

The standard of TC was purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium citrate (AC), L-cysteine (Cys), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl-dimethoxymethylsilane (AEAPMS), ethanol, toluene, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), acetone, methanol, dichloromethane, ammonium hydroxide, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrogen chloride (HCl) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), styrene, acrylic acid (AA), ammonium persulfate (APS), dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was obtained from Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, USA). All reagents used in the experiments are at least analytical grade.

Stock solution of TC (2 mg L−1) was prepared in 1:4 methanol/H2O and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. PBS of different pH was prepared with NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaOH, HCl and ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AEAPMS functionalized carbon dots (AEAPMS-CD)
ce:section.ce:para: N and S co-doped carbon dots (NSCD) was synthesized according to our previous work [15]. In brief, AC (2 g) and Cys (1 g) were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and treated ultrasonically for 2 min. The transparent solution was heated by a household microwave oven at 750 W for 2.5 min. The resulting product was dissolved in ethanol and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The final purified NSCD was dispersed in 100 mL ethanol (18.54 mg mL−1). A mixture of AEAPMS (3 mL) and ethanol solution of NSCD (30 mL) was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 30 min. After irradiation by a household microwave oven operating at 375 W for 4 min, the resulting AEAPMS-CD was cooled to room temperature for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles were prepared by emulsion polymerization. In a typical synthesis, 2 mL styrene was added into a ethanol/H2O (5:2, 84 mL) mixture contained 100 mg SDS to form a microemulsion. Next, 3 mL styrene and 1 mL AA were added dropwise. Then, 80 mg APS was added to initiate polymerization. The polymerization reaction was carried out at 70 °C purged with nitrogen gas for 12 h. The resulting carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles were washed with ethanol and ultrapure water several times to remove unreacted precursors. The final carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles were separated by centrifugation and dried at 40 °C.

Carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particles (200 mg) were suspended in 1% SDS aqueous solution (50 mL) under ultrasonic vibration (5 min). DBP (1 mL) was added into the above emulsion and the first swelling step was carried out under microwave irradiation at 60 °C for 1 h. Then, the second swelling step was carried out for another 1 h at 60 °C after the addition of the AEAPMS-CD, APTES (3 mL), TC (100 mg) and toluene (8 mL). Subsequently, 10 mL TEOS and 5 mL 25% ammonium hydroxide were slowly dripped into the mixture and stirred under room temperature for 30 min. The prepolymerization was performed at 40 °C for 30 min, and then the polymerization was carried out at 60 °C for 1 h. The product was further aged at 80 °C for 30 min. The resulting product was separated by centrifugation and washed with ethanol. Finally, the HMIP@CD was obtained by removing the polystyrene particles and template molecules (TC) using dichloromethane and acetone. The HMIP@CD was dried at 60 °C and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to use. All the heating process was performed using a MDS-6G high-pressure microwave oven.

For comparison, the hollow non-imprinted polymers embedded carbon dots (HNIP@CD) was prepared in the same way as HMIP@CD except not adding template. The surface molecularly imprinted polymers embedded carbon dots (SMIP@CD) was also prepared following the same procedure as HMIP@CD except that the polystyrene particles were not removed.

The morphologies of NSCD and HMIP@CD was characterized by a JEM-2100 transmission election microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and a JEM-6700F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra (4000–400 cm−1) in KBr were obtained from a Nicolet AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Madison, USA). XRD patterns were recorded using a XRD-6100 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analysis was performed with a Micromeritics Autochem II 2920 analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The thermogravimetry analysis (TG) at 30–800 °C was carried out on a TGA Q500 Thermal Analyzer Instrument (TA, USA) at the heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. The fluorescence spectra were measured by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan).

HMIP@CD (1 mg) was dispersed in 5 mL PBS (pH 7, 0.1 M). After addition of different amount of TC, the mixture was diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water. The resulting solution was shaken for 3 min and the HMIP@CD was isolated by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 5 min. The isolated HMIP@CD was redispersed in 5 mL PBS (pH 7). Then, the mixture was diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water and the fluorescence spectrum of each solution was measured form 435–750 nm with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set to 10 nm and the response time was set to 0.25 s.

Honey samples were purchased from a local supermarket (Changchun, China). Honey sample (1 g) was diluted with 5 mL of PBS (pH 7). The diluted honey sample was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and filtrated though a 0.22 µm membrane filter to remove solid suspension. HMIP@CD (1 mg) was dispersed in the diluted honey sample. The following fluorescence detection is same as described in Section 2.6.

The preparation process of HMIP@CD is schematically depicted in Fig. 1A. NSCD with carboxyl groups was prepared from AC and Cys via a simple microwave-assisted method. After fabrication and swelling of the carboxyl functionalized polystyrene particle, a homogenous cross-linking polymer shell was formed at the surface of the polystyrene particle by microphase separation and volume shrinkage of the polymer layer [16]. HMIP@CD was obtained through the removal of polystyrene particle with dichloromethane [17]. During the reaction, AEAPMS-CD was covalently embedded into HMIP@CD through the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of APTES and TEOS via microwave-assisted sol-gel method. Compared with conventional hydrothermal method which needs at least 2 days to prepare HMIP [18,19], the polymerization process in this study can be accomplished within only 4 h.

Fig. 2A shows the TEM image of NSCD. As shown in the histograms (inset of Fig. 2A), the diameter of NSCD ranges from 1 nm to 4 nm. The morphology of HMIP@CD was characterized by SEM (Fig. 2B). The spherical HMIP@CD shows a single-hole hollow structure with a rough surface and its diameter is 2–4 µm. The hollow structure greatly enhances the specific surface area of HMIP@CD. Compared with traditional MIP, the surface imprinting sites of HMIP@CD are more, which improves the mass transfer rate and sensitivity [20].

Fig. 2C shows the FT-IR spectrum of HMIP@CD. The broad peaks at 3300 cm−1 and 1646 cm−1 are related to N − H group [21]. The peak around 2932 cm−1 is attributed to C−H stretching vibration [22]. The intensive peak at 1065 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of Si−O−Si band [23]. The peaks located at 795 cm−1 and 451 cm−1 represent the vibration of Si−O band [24]. These results demonstrate the successful synthesis of HMIP@CD, which is generated from sol-gel condensation of APTES and TEOS. The FT-IR spectra of PS particle is shown in Fig. S1.
The XRD pattern of HMIP@CD in Fig. 2D shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.1°, which is close to that of NSCD (2θ = 24.7°), revealing that the NSCD has been successfully doped into the HMIP@CD.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments (Fig. S2) demonstrate that the surface area of HMIP@CD is 349.4 m2 g−1. The HMIP@CD has relatively higher surface area compared to SMIP@CD (299.7 m2 g−1) and HNIP@CD (323.2 m2 g−1). The porous structure of HMIP@CD benefits for mass transfer and specific recognition of TC.

TG experiment was performed to study the thermal stability behavior of HMIP@CD. The TG-DTG profile of HMIP@CD in a temperature range from 30° to 800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere is displayed in Fig. 3A. The HMIP@CD has an initial slight mass loss of about 4.5% from 30° to 125 °C, which can be attributed to the loss of solvent and water [25]. The mass loss between 125 and 400 °C is primarily due to the elimination of water from interior or the condensation of hydroxyl group. The obvious mass loss starts from 400 °C is attributed to the decomposition of imprinted polymer coatings [26]. The three peaks of DTG curve in 30–800 °C are approximately consistent with the three mass losses of TG curve. The trend of mass loss in SMIP@CD (Fig. 3B) is consistent with that of HMIP@CD except that the total weight loss (34.1%) is higher than that of HMIP@CD (24.5%). Such distinction is attributed to the decomposition of polystyrene particle embedded in SMIP@CD. These results further indicate that the HMIP@CD has been prepared successfully with high thermal stability [27].

Adsorption isotherms provide important theoretical parameters to assess the adsorption mechanism of TC to the adsorbent. Fig. S3 shows the adsorption isotherms of HMIP@CD (SMIP@CD/HNIP@CD) for TC. The experimental data was fitted by Langmuir and Freundlich models (Fig. S4), which are suitable for monolayer adsorption with homogeneous adsorption sites and heterogeneous surface adsorption with multiple binding sites, respectively. The calculated parameters for Langmuir and Freundlich models are summarized in Table S1. For the HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD, Langmuir isotherm model (R2 =0.979, 0.987 and 0.992) fitted better than Freundlich model (R2 =0.977, 0.975 and 0.988), which indicates that Langmuir model fits the adsorption process of HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD for TC well. The results imply the homogeneous distribution of adsorption sites on the surface of HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD [28]. The maximum adsorption capacity of HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD are calculated to be 6.93, 5.76 and 4.05 mg g−1, respectively, which is quite close to the experimental data.

As depicted in Fig. S5, the Scatchard plots of the HMIP@CD and SMIP@CD are composed of two curves with different slope, which represent the nonspecific and specific interaction between TC and HMIP@CD/SMIP@CD. Inset of Fig. S5 shows that the Scatchard plot of HNIP@CD contains only one curve, which demonstrates the nonspecific absorption nature between TC and HNIP@CD. The calculated parameters are shown in Table S2.

To further evaluate the kinetics of the adsorption, the kinetic profiles of HMIP@CD was investigated in 30 mg L−1 TC solution using indirect UV–Vis to detect the free TC in supernate. As can be seen in Fig. S6, the adsorption rate of HMIP@CD is fast in first 20 min and gradually reached equilibrium at 30 min. However, it takes 90 min for the SMIP@CD to reached equilibrium, which demonstrates the faster mass transfer rate of HMIP@CD compared to SMIP@CD. The equilibrium time of HNIP@CD (20 min) is less due to the lack of imprinted sites.

As shown in Fig. S7, the experimental date fitted pseudo-second-order model (R2 = 0.992, 0.993 and 0.996) better than pseudo-first-order model (R2 = 0.726, 0.933 and 0.397). The Q
e calculated from pseudo-second-order model for HMIP@CD (SMIP@CD/HNIP@CD) are closer to the experimental value (Table S3). It is indicated that the adsorption processes of HMIP@CD, SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD are mainly dominated by chemisorption, involving sharing of electrons between sorbent and adsorbate [29].

The optical properties of HMIP@CD were studied (Fig. S8). The excitation and emission wavelength of HMIP@CD are 390 nm and 503 nm, respectively. There is some redshift of the excitation and emission spectra of HMIP@CD compared to that of NSCD. Fluorescence of CDs is correlated with its surface state [30]. During the bonding process, template, monomer, and crosslinker have caused the surface changes of NSCD (such as protonation or deprotonation) [31], and also increases the particle size of NSCD, resulting in a red shift of the excitation-emission spectra [32].

The photostability of HMIP@CD was studied by recording its fluorescence intensity every 5 min. As shown in Fig. S9, the HMIP@CD exhibits almost the same fluorescence intensity for 60 min, which indicates the stable fluorescence emission of HMIP@CD. After preserved at 4 °C for two months, the HMIP@CD powders exhibit 94% of initial fluorescence intensity with no shift of the fluorescence peak after dispersing in PBS.

The effect of pH (1−14) on the fluorescence of HMIP@CD was studied in the presence and absence of TC as displayed in Fig. S10. The fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD is relatively lower in acidic media which is attributed to the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of NSCD in acidic condition [33]. As pH increases, the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD increases. Under alkaline conditions, functional groups on the surface of NSCD are deprotonated and a negative charged protective layer is formed on the surface of NSCD. The negatively charged protective layer protect the HMIP@CD against alkali attack, and the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD shows no significant decrease in alkaline condition [34,35]. The highest fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F) is achieved at pH 7, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD in the absence and presence of TC, respectively. However, the fluorescence quenching rate decreases in higher pH range. Such pH-dependent fluorescence response is probably due to the denaturation of TC in high pH [36]. Therefore, the detection is performed at pH 7.

The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of HMIP@CD by TC (100 μg L−1) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, the HMIP@CD shows rapid fluorescence quenching within 3 min and the signal is stable over a time period of 90 min. However, the SMIP@CD requires 10 min to achieve stability. It is demonstrated that the adsorption rate of HMIP@CD is faster than that of SMIP@CD. Such a fast adsorption rate is assigned to the favorable hollow structure and high surface area of HMIP@CD.

Electron transfer from the HMIP@CD (donor) to TC (acceptor) is considered as the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence emission spectrum of HMIP@CD and the UV–Vis spectra of TC and HMIP@CD are shown in Fig. 4B. There is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of TC and the fluorescence emission spectrum of HMIP@CD. Therefore, fluorescence quenching is not induced by fluorescence resonant energy transfer [37]. The overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC and HMIP@CD allows charge transfer from the conduction band of HMIP@CD to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of TC and leads to the fluorescence quenching [9].

As shown in Fig. 4C, after template extraction, the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD (curve a) is restored (curve b). With the addition of TC (100 μg L−1), the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD is dramatically quenched (curve c). However, when the HMIP@CD is applied to the detection of TC in honey sample (100 μg L−1), the fluorescence spectrum of HMIP@CD is totally covered by the autofluorescence of honey sample (curve d), which makes it hard to detect TC in honey sample by direct fluorescence strategy. To solve this problem, the HMIP@CD was first dispersed in diluted honey sample. After adsorbing TC, the HMIP@CD was separated by centrifugation and redispersed in PBS (pH 7) for fluorescence detection. The experimental process is shown in Fig. 1B. Satisfactory recovery was acquired after the separation and redispersion process (curve e), which demonstrates that the method can be effectively applied to detect TC in honey sample.

The determination ability of HMIP@CD for TC was evaluated under optimum conditions. The fluorescence spectra of HMIP@CD with different concentrations of TC are presented in Fig. S11A. The fluorescence detection of SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD for TC was performed for comparison (Fig. S11B,C). The extent of fluorescence quenching of HMIP@CD is much larger than that of SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD. The results indicate that the HMIP@CD exhibits stronger binding capacity and higher sensitivity than SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD, which is owing to the hollow structure. The fluorescence quenching of the materials toward TC can be quantified by the Stern-Volmer equation:

K
SV
C
TC
+ 1
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescence materials in the absence and presence of TC, respectively. K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C
TC is the concentration of TC. The fluorescence quenching rate (F
0
/F) of the materials is linearly associated with the concentration of TC in the range of 10–200 μg L−1 and the Stern-Volmer plots satisfy the following equations:
(2)
HMIP @ CD :
F

= 0.0044

(3)

(4)

= 0.9545 )

Imprinting factor (IF), which is calculated as the ratio of K
SV,MIP to K
SV,NIP , is used to evaluate the specific recognition ability of MIPs. The IF of HMIP@CD is calculated to be 6.28, which implies the effective imprinting and specific recognition effect of HMIP@CD. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of HMIP@CD is much higher than that of SMIP@CD and HNIP@CD. The limit of detection (LOD) of HMIP@CD is 3.1 μg L−1 calculated according to 3σ criteria.

The reproducibility of HMIP@CD was evaluated. The removal-rebinding cycles were repeated six times using the same HMIP@CD. During the processes, the fluorescence of HMIP@CD was recovered by elution of the absorbed TC with acetone for 10 min Fig. S12 shows the fluorescence intensity of HMIP@CD before and after the adsorption of TC (100 μg L−1). The HMIP@CD retains 96.5% of its original fluorescence intensity after six recycles. The results demonstrate that the HMIP@CD can be repeatedly used at least six times without a significant decrease in the determination capacity for TC.

Five batches of HMIP@CD were prepared, and there are a difference of 2–5 nm in fluorescence emission wavelength and a 2–4% difference in fluorescence emission intensity. To obtain more accurate results, the same batch of HMIP@CD was used throughout the experiments.

To evaluate the selectivity of HMIP@CD, TC analogue oxytetracycline (OTC), and other antibiotics with similar structure including florfenicol (FF), enrofloxacin (ENR) and sulfamonomethoxine (SMM) were selected and measured. Their chemical structures are shown in Fig. S13. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the specific recognition ability of HMIP@CD for OTC and other antibiotics with similar structure (FF, ENR and SMM) is not good. This is because the imprinting sites in HMIP@CD are not completely complementary to OTC. For other structural similar antibiotics, not only the imprinting sites are not complementary, but also the shapes of imprinting cavities are not matched. Due to the lack of imprinting sites, there is not much difference between the fluorescence response of HNIP@CD to the template and other reference compound.

There are usually some co-existing potentially interfering species in honey sample, including common metal ions (Ni2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, PO4 3- and Cl-), amino acids (tryptophan, proline, phenylalanine, methionine, valine, threonine, lysine, dl-leucine, arginine, asparaginic acid, tyrosine and cysteine), saccharides (glucose, fructose and sucrose) and vitamins (vitamin B1 and ascorbic acid). Interference study was performed in the presence of each co-existing substance. It can be seen in Table S4, the co-existing substances in honey have no obvious influence on the detection of TC under the selected conditions, which demonstrates that the HMIP@CD is suitable for the determination of TC in honey sample.

The method based on HMIP@CD was applied to detect TC in honey samples. As no TC was detected in honey samples, a recovery study was performed. The results are summarized in Table 1. The recoveries obtained by the proposed method are between 93% and 105% with RSDs of 0.6–1.6%.

Table 2 shows the comparison of the proposed method with other reported methods for fluorescence determination of TC. The results indicate that the LOD, recovery and RSD of the proposed method are comparable with those reported methods. However, those methods were used for detection of samples with no interference of autofluorescence. So far, there was no reports on the use of CDs to detect TC in real sample with autofluorescence. The method based on HMIP@CD has accomplished the selective fluorescence determination of TC in honey sample with autofluorescence interference for the first time.

The HMIP@CD was prepared through a microwave-assisted sol-gel method and the preparation time was significantly shortened. The HMIP@CD has several advantages over traditional solid core MIP@CD, including high surface area, fast adsorption rate and high sensitivity. The fluorescence detection method for TC in honey was constructed based on HMIP@CD, which has avoided the interference caused by the autofluorescence of honey sample. This method will hopefully extend MIP@CD to the determination of abundant analytes in complicated samples which have interference of autofluorescence.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.04.024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright multicolor bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum dots for electroluminescent light-emitting diodes
